speaker: interviewer
text: You say the Lithuanians had been preparing for this time. Why do you say that? How do you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because Lithuania, [PAUSES FOR SECONDS] I hate to say that, is one of the most anti-Semitic countries, little countries, in the world, Lithuania. What-- what's the reason? We don't know. But Lithuanians it all these years hate against the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience any of that before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: When you used to go from school home, we used to be stoned by Gentile kids. We had to run. When there used to be a soccer game between Maccabi and the Lithuanian commanders, you know, teams, in soccer, and if the Jews were about to win, we had to run from the stadium very fast. Otherwise, we were beaten and stoned again. If a Jewish boxer knocked out a Gentile Lithuanian in the ring, the people outside the stadium-- the-- the boxing arena was in danger already. With clubs and stones, and the fights used to break out terrible. And Lithuanians, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] even if they are a Catholic country, you know, the Lithuania. And for [INAUDIBLE] the old, I-- I think, they'll all thinking that the Jews are at fault of Jesus' death. And this is the old fanatic Lithuanians who couldn't get away from that, that we killed Jesus.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any authorities ever come to the aid of the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: When it was real bad, they came. But they saw a fight where a little Gentile Lithuanian, beating up a Jewish Lithuanian, they looked a little on the side, another couple of punches wouldn't hurt.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you handle that personally? What-- what were you thinking?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was thinking all the time, that's how we live. That's, it's our country. I was born here. It's Lithuania. Maybe one of these days, they are gonna get more educated, more sensible, more modern, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and understand more, all about what life is, that we are all human beings. And have-- we are under the same God, you know. And we have the same right to live. But it didn't-- it didn't come so fast. That even old Gentile people with crosses wearing, they still hated Jews. [PAUSES FOR  SECONDS] That's right. And to say the truth, you know, it was very hard to make friends with Lithuanians. Even if they are friendly with you on the outside, but in-- in their hearts, they were not friends. And that's [?why?] when they made the pograms, and the Germans were already you know it was a free city, Kaunas in Slobodka. The Russians were already escaping, you know, chasing away. Because the Germans came in with such a power, they bombed the airfields. And the Russians, the soldiers were not organized no more. They didn't see the officers anymore. It was a chaos in the-- in the Soviet army because of the speed of Hitler's war. It was so organized that they were off balanced. And the Lithuanians right away took that for a good opportunity to start killing. So before even the Germans marched in, and it was almost like a free city, you know, only a couple of Russian soldiers who got lost didn't know where to run. They were confused. [? They had ?] no officers. The Lithuanians though saw how they-- they killed them, the-- the Soviet army soldiers, the Russians. So you see how the hatred worked against. And when the Germans were all heading to Lithuanian, they didn't tell the Lithuanians, go kill the Jews. They did it by them self, happy, And they did the first pograms. And they killed thousand Jews in Slobodka. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] With knives and ax, they knocked off people's Jewish heads.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see this happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother went to a bakery. My mother said, go [INAUDIBLE] Abraham to the bakery by Sandler. Maybe you can get some bread left over there. My brother walked into the bakery. It was all dark. And when he walked in, on the ground, on the floor, was laying Mr. Sandler and Mrs. Sandler, and their sons. They're all laying in a puddle of blood. And all the throats were sliced.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he came back home and he says, Mama, the Sandlers are all dead. They are laying all in blood. And my mother says, don't talk like that. How do you know? Maybe you didn't see good? He says, Mother, I saw it. And my mother said, it's bitter, kinder. Children, it's bitter. We go to start hiding ourselves.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was the big, you know, pogram made by the Lithuanians. Not even one German took act in the pogram. The Germans took later their business, systematically according to the books, and according to the orders of the system how to kill all the Jews, you know, to exterminate the Jews. All with a system, all things. But the Lithuanians, like butchers, they slaughter us. And it was two groups of Lithuanians who did the job. One group was educated, college Lithuanians who killed Jews, slaughtered them in the houses. And they walked away from the houses. The other group Lithuanians, they killed the Jews and they robbed them. They came with little wagons, with horses, and they start taking out furniture, the jewelry, and all valuables in the house. So there were two groups in the pogram. The educated killed and walked away, with guns, with pistols. The other group was Lithuanians robbed and slaughtered like in butchers and-- and killed the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was-- was this the local population? Did you know any of these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we knew plenty. Because in my grandmother's house, my grandmother had a cellar. And all the family went in-- into the cellar to hide.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Not my family. We were still, we were not living near my grandmother. My grandmother lived on [INAUDIBLE] Gasse, [INAUDIBLE] Street, which is a real Jewish section, the same thing like in New York, the [INAUDIBLE] Street. [LAUGHS] The same was [INAUDIBLE] Gasse, like the [INAUDIBLE] Street. And they were all hiding in that cellar. And the cover of the cellar was covered with a carpet like in my house here. And a table was after they went in, they let down the thing with the table and chairs around. And when the pogram started and the Lithuanians start to kill, house to house and [INAUDIBLE] Gasse people, the woman, Gentile woman who used to help my grandmother sometimes, and my grandmother treated her really well. She used to eat by the same table with my grandmother and grandfather and with my all uncles for years and years. She came in with a lot of the Lithuanians hooligans, partisans. And she heard, she heard the voice of Janova that was her name, the woman who was taking care of my grandmother's house many years and helping her. And she said looks like nobody is here. Maybe they are in the synagogue. In the new synagogue, there was the name is [INAUDIBLE] Shul. The Gentile woman said to other partisans, the Lithuanians, let's go there. They must, they must all hiding themselves in the-- in the new synagogue. And they all went to the new synagogue. And there was a lot of the Jews hiding really there. And they slaughtered all the Jews in that synagogue. And that was by a woman who knew all the people in Slobodka, especially on [INAUDIBLE] Gasse.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your grandmother tell you that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my grandmother tell me that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think your family was saved?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say it was pure luck. If we would have lived on [INAUDIBLE] Gasse, we would have been slaughtered the same thing like the thousands other Jews were slaughtered. But we lived at the outskirts between a lot of, you know, factories was a all around there. Maybe we were saved, but it was a time my mother got scared already too. And she start, you know to separate some of us, the children. And in the beginning, we thought that it's going to be more safe-- safer to live in the Jewish section than in the Gentile where we lived, you know, on the outskirts. So for a couple of days, my mother sent me away to my grandmother to live. And that was actually a day before the pogram, a day or two days before the pograms. But the Germans were already in Lithuania.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the Germans arriving in Lithuania?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, when I was in my grandmother's, when I was in my grandmother's house, sleep over the night. And that was May, I mean, that was June the 26th. I got up-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was the 25th, I'm sorry, 25th in the morning. I heard, you know, noise. And I went in my grandmother's yard. And a ball, like a soccer ball, over the fence fell in right in my grandmother's yard. So I look at the ball, and I opened up the gate. And I saw all the trucks, the German trucks, the army trucks, and all ammunitions. And the all, the part of the German army was already in [INAUDIBLE] Gasse. And the Germans were sitting on the trucks with the shiny boots, with the fancy uniforms, drinkin' champagne. And the Lithuanian Gentile girls greeting them with flowers. Collaborators right away with the German army. And kissing and hugging. And the trucks were clean and the uniforms.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I couldn't believe. I said, how could the army march in the war time without a shot, you know. How did it came in so fast? With no, I mean, in a war. I thought that's the most powerful army in the world. My heart was crying. I couldn't believe. I thought they are gonna take over the world.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were giggling, and smiling, and clean, and drinkin' champagne, and eatin' chocolate, and being kissed by the girls, the Lithuanians. And then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] while I was still looking in out through the gate, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they grabbed a lost Soviet soldier, who didn't know. Remember, I told you, it was a chaos. And a lot of the soldiers got lost. They didn't know where to run. And one soldier was hiding himself behind the corner house there with a machine gun. And German airplanes [POINTING] were starting already to come, fly around, over the-- the-- the Slobodka, in the skies. And that's a Russian soldier, with a machine gun, try to shoot down the airplane. And the soldier was like a Mongolian, you know, from Russia, you know, from that descent, from this. Russia has, you know, all different type soldier from different place of the country. But I saw on his face as he grabbed him and dragged him right across the street of my grandmother's house, a big officer, a German. I don't know his rank, but he was one of the biggest. When the soldiers, the German grabbed him. They brought him right to that [POINTS] big officer. And the officer called him like that, closer to him with his finger. And the Russian soldier walked closer and closer, and the German took out a sword. And he chopped off his head in front of me while I was looking through the gate. When I saw that, I run into my grandmother. And I was crying. I couldn't stop. And I says Grandma, I just saw the German chopping off the Russian soldier's head. Look it, right there. Look at there, near Glick's house. A man lived there, Glick. Right near Glick's house. She says, don't talk silly things like that. Maybe you didn't see anything. I says, I saw, Grandma. And then I decided I'm going back, try to go back home. When I saw the army coming in, that German army, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I thought there was no future no more. I thought the whole world is gonna be taken by Hitler. Because I've never seen such a power with, such a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] weapons, with such a organization. They smashed, you know, the Lithuania-- the Lithuanians. They smashed the Russian army so fast, that they grabbed thousands and thousands, tens of thousands prisoners. They didn't have a chance to escape even, the Russian army from Lithuania. And they were grabbed, you know, all over you know, as prisoners. And that was every other big disappointment for me I thought it's-- there's no chance. But I saw the chance. And we'll talk later. I saw the shining German army. And then three years later, I've seen the German helmet laying in the gutter and they couldn't walk without shoes. But by the same Soviet army was chasing him and they didn't know how to run. And they were laying in the street. I saw both sides, but it took three and a half years of punishment, killed out plenty Jews, till they saw what a change it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went back home to your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, went back home from my grandmother's house. And I went back to our house. And I said to my mother what I just saw. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's exactly like our papa said, our father said. Exactly what he was saying, it's happening. And my mother couldn't stop crying.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you learn that your father's fate?

speaker: interviewee
text: About my father's fate, I learned almost nine months later. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A man was released from the ninth fortress where all the Jews from the-- from Slobodka, Kaunas, they're all taken to the Seventh Fort, sorry, not the Ninth, Seventh fortress. They were all taken from the streets and from their homes, from [INAUDIBLE], Slobodka-- taken all to the Seventh Fort to be killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one of my father's friends, he was a cabinet maker. And his name was Melamed. He was in that fort. And he had a friend, a Lithuanian, he took part of killing, you know, with the other Lithuanians, the Jews, was a friend with that Jew, Melamed. He built for him furniture a lot of times. And he said to him before, shooting, you know, groups of people. The ways that they do to killing is groups, you know, like 50 at a time, or 30.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was almost at the grave to be shot, that Lithuanian partisan-- we called them partisans, the Lithuanians because they acted like partisans, you know, against the Russians and against the-- against the Russians of course. He recognized he says, Melamed. And he-- he stopped, you know, gave the order not to shoot that group people. And he called out, Melamed, the cabinet maker. And he gave an order to release him, take him away from the Seventh Fort. And when Melamed came in the ghetto, when we had already the Ghetto. And I was talking to him. And he knew my father well. In conversation, he threw in words and he skipped out. And he said, your father and your cousin, Mesaleh Leibke, they were shot on the Seventh Fort.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he didn't really want to tell you.

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't want to tell me but, in conversation, he-- he told me. But till I finded out, till I found out, I thought any day, my father is coming back. We didn't know he was shot. So near my house was a two-story house, friends lived, a Jewish family, Arnofsky. So I used to go up on the second floor, all the time, every day, before we went into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I used to look through that little window, because from her house was a big field. And when we used to walk home, we used to cut through through that field. So I used to look at every day through that window if my father is coming. And when I saw from far away a man, you know, with the short build, stocky, from far away, oh, my father is it? And when it came closer, it was not my father. It's somebody, a Gentile man or a farmer.

speaker: interviewee
text: And for weeks and weeks, I used to climb up in [INAUDIBLE] second floor and look through the window and wanted to see if my father is coming back. Because it was rumored that a lot of the Jews were caught, and they were digging, you know, [?sod?] you know, like we are putting on the lawn, here in, this in Miami, these were digging [? sod ?] in a small little town out of Kaunas called [INAUDIBLE]. It was just a rumor. And there's a lot of Jews working there. See that, they grabbed a lot of Jews from the rail-- railroad station, and they took them to [INAUDIBLE]. So I thought my father was in [INAUDIBLE] too, and he is going to come any day home. And till then, even till the ghetto, I used to climb up on the second floor and look and see if my father's coming back. I can never forget that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the Jewish community in anyway unified at this time? Was there any leadership?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not till we went into the ghetto. Because a lot of the rabbis and big leaders were already taken to the forts too, to the fortress, you know, to be killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think they purposely tried to get the-- the leadership of the community first? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: They took-- the real leadership and educated Jews were taken in, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] were taken away when the ghetto was closed. Then they could really surround them, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you understand?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you, how did the ghetto begin?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the ghetto was closed, when the ghetto was closed, August 17th, 1941. The ghetto was closed August 17th, 1941. The ghetto was closed, with-- with the-- with all the wires and everything. Then the Germans gave an order-- there was already Jewish committee in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's-- let's backtrack one minute. The Germans came in June, 1941.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in June--

speaker: interviewer
text: And the ghetto was established by August?

speaker: interviewee
text: August the 15th.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know about the ghetto? What-- what--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was already they start to grab Jews, to dig holes and start building the fences around a certain neighborhood where the ghetto was established to being made. The Germans already and the Lithuanians all together start to lay out. They made a plan which sections are gonna be in-- in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto was in Slobodka, not in Kaunas. And it started two or three streets in of [INAUDIBLE] Strasse, [INAUDIBLE] Gasse. And it took a lot of, a lot of homes, you know. It surrounded I cannot mention how many streets, how many-- how many sections. But in the beginning of the ghetto, I think was about 25,000 or 30,000 Jews in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the ghetto, was it established in what was a Jewish section of town?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was most of the Jewish sections and a small part of they took it away from the Gentile sections, but a very small part. But then the Gentile sections was given back, because they made selections. And they killed many Jews, so the ghetto became smaller. So the ghetto, the Gentile people start to get back, you know, their homes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We're going to stop now for a minute.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
